entry.title: Question about Functional Location and Equipment Hierarchy comparison and best practice
entry.content:
Hi everyone, I have some questions regarding FLOC–Equipment.

First of all, I’m working as an instrument engineer (user side), so I’m not very familiar with SAP best practices. However, I’d like to compare the structure in my current company with my previous one.

In my former company, the functional location–equipment hierarchy looked like this:
S-01-UTL-060-01
(S = Company; 01 = Plant; UTL = Utility area; 060 = Unit 600—e.g., Air Separation Unit; 01 = Air Compressor Area)

The equipment hierarchy assigned there was, for example:
Main Equipment: S-060-C01 (Air Compressor)
Sub-equipment: S-060-FT-002

Technically, 060-FT-002 is not part of the compressor itself (it is installed on the discharge pipe to its consumer). However, it is assigned there because it makes systematic review easier.

The reporting, planning, and overall structure feel very intuitive and logical to me.

In contrast, my current company’s structure is as follows:

FLOC:
HM-UMT-UAS-AG01
(HM = Company; UMT = Company unit; UAS = Plant; AG01 = Unit—e.g., Plant Air)

The equipment assigned here consists only of major mechanical equipment, with no sub-equipment. Additionally, there are separate functional locations:
HM-UMT-UAS-AG01-INST (for instrumentation)
HM-UMT-UAS-AG01-ELC (for electrical)

In my view, this is not intuitive, and we are not utilizing SAP as effectively as we could, or as I understand it should be used.

I am considering proposing a restructuring of the hierarchy to be more similar to my previous company.

My questions are:

  1. Is it common practice to separate functional locations and equipment based on discipline? If so, what are the reasons or advantages compared to a more integrated structure like the one in my previous company?
  2. Where should instruments be placed—as main equipment or as sub-equipment—even if, technically, they are not part of the main equipment, as in the example above?

 

 

Thanks,

Ben

entry.title: Issue with Japanese IME Input Recognition in Value Help Search Field (Fiori Elements)
entry.content:
In a SAP Fiori Elements application running on SAPUI5 1.120.2, using OData V4 and CAPM (Nodejs) as backend service, we implement Value Help using annotations only (@Common.ValueList).


When users input Japanese text using IME in the search field inside the Value Help and perform conversion (space + enter), the latest converted value is not recognized by the search field for the first time. If user closes the Value Help dialog and enters the input again for the second time, the search field works as expected.


Unexpected result: 

  • The result list is not updated according to the latest input.

Expected Result: 

  • The result list should be updated immediately based on the final converted Japanese text, without requiring the dialog to be reopened.
Question:  Has anyone experienced this issue before? If so, how was it resolved? Any guidance would be appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Event-driven architecture
entry.content:
Thanks, @VijayKonam  — that helps. Let me add one more angle from a Utilities perspective because the patterns get tricky at scale.

In Utilities, a single event (billing, meter read, device update, credit action, move‑in/out) usually drives multiple downstream behaviors with different latency, enrichment, and retry needs. That’s where I’m still trying to draw a clean boundary between what AEM should handle vs. what belongs in a consumer service.

AEM gives me the backbone — routing, fan‑out, durability. But I still need a place for shared enrichment (customer tier, SLA, premise attributes), validation, idempotency, and business‑specific error handling. If every consumer re‑implements that, it becomes hard to maintain.

So the open question I’m exploring is:

Do we keep consumers very thin and let each one handle its own logic, or do we introduce a small domain service that does shared enrichment before fanning out to specialized consumers?

If anyone has implemented this in a high‑volume industry (Utilities, Retail, Telco), I’d love to hear how you avoided duplicated logic and kept the consumer side clean.

entry.title: Re: SAP UI5 - QUnit-Testing
entry.content: Nice blog!

entry.title: Re: pass and manage complex dynamic selections
entry.content:
I want to bring the question back with a slightly sharper angle because this comes up a lot in real projects.

My main concern is how to handle complex FREE_SELECTIONS + stable output consumption when wrapping standard reports. In many cases, the selection logic becomes dynamic and multi‑layered, and the ALV output structures change across upgrades. Both together make the wrapper fragile.

I’m curious how others are handling this in practice: What’s the most stable pattern you’ve used to pass dynamic selections and consume report output without breaking after upgrades? If you’ve solved this in a large implementation (Utilities, Retail, Finance), I’d really like to hear what worked.

entry.title: Re: standard SAP report logic
entry.content:
I want to expand this question a bit because this comes up often in real projects. When we try to reuse standard SAP report logic inside custom ABAP programs, the choice is not always clear: Should we rely on SUBMIT + CL_SALV_BS_RUNTIME_INFO, or should we avoid ALV capture entirely and use released APIs/CDS views instead?

The challenge is that many standard reports contain valuable business logic, but their ALV output structures are not stable across upgrades. In industries like Utilities or Finance, even a small field change can break downstream processes.

I’m trying to understand what others consider the most reliable long‑term pattern: reuse via SUBMIT, rebuild logic using CDS views/APIs, or a hybrid approach. If you’ve dealt with this in a large implementation, I’d appreciate your perspective on what worked and what didn’t.

entry.title: Dynamic Handling of Variants in ABAP – Save, Load, Delete
entry.content:
Introduction--
In SAP, variants are used to save input values for selection screens of reports or programs. Instead of re-entering the same parameters every time, a user can simply select a variant and execute the report.
Normally, variants are created manually via the Selection Screen → Save As Variant option. But in some cases, we need to create, update, or delete variants dynamically in programmatically — for example: 

  • Automating recurring report runs. 
  • Delivering pre-defined variants to users. 
  • Handling variants dynamically during background job scheduling. 

This blog explains how to handle variants end-to-end in ABAP using function modules.

Technical Background-- 

Key Tables / Structures-- 

  • VARIANT -- Holds the variant name (type RSVARIANT-VARIANT). 
  • RSPARAMS -- Holds selection-screen parameters and select-options. 
  • VARID / VARIT -- Holds variant metadata (header + text). 

Function Modules Used-- 

  • RS_REFRESH_FROM_SELECTOPTIONS – Capture current selection-screen values into an internal table. 
  • RS_CREATE_VARIANT – Create a new variant with captured values. 
  • RS_VARIANT_CATALOG – Display a pop-up to choose a variant. 
  • RS_SUPPORT_SELECTIONS – Apply a variant to current selection screen. 
  • RS_VARIANT_DELETE – Delete an existing variant.

Demo Program Walkthrough-- 

We built a small demo report ZBRT_RP_VARIENT with:

  • A selection screen (fields from SFLIGHT table + variant name). 
  • Three function keys: Save Variant, Load Variant, Delete Variant. 
  • Subroutines that handle each operation.
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report ZBRT_RP_VARIENT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Demo for creating, reading, deleting and applying variants dynamically
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
REPORT zbrt_rp_varient.

TABLES: sflight,
        sscrfields.

" --- Selection screen fields ------------------------------------------------
SELECTION-SCREEN BEGIN OF BLOCK b1 WITH FRAME TITLE TEXT-001.
  PARAMETERS: p_carrid TYPE sflight-carrid,
              p_connid TYPE sflight-connid,
              p_fldate TYPE sflight-fldate.
  PARAMETERS: p_vname  TYPE variant LOWER CASE.    " Variant name input
SELECTION-SCREEN END OF BLOCK b1.

" Add function keys (Save / Load / Delete)
SELECTION-SCREEN: FUNCTION KEY 1, FUNCTION KEY 2, FUNCTION KEY 3.

INITIALIZATION.
  " Function key labels for UX
  sscrfields-functxt_01 = 'Save Variant'.
  sscrfields-functxt_02 = 'Load Variant'.
  sscrfields-functxt_03 = 'Delete Variant'.

  " --- Variables -------------------------------------------------------------
  DATA: gv_repid   TYPE sy-repid VALUE sy-repid,
        gv_variant LIKE rsvar-variant VALUE 'ZVAR1',                " variant name
        lv_var_des LIKE varid,
        lt_text    TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF varit,
        lt_params  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF rsparams WITH EMPTY KEY.

  " --- Handle function keys / variant actions --------------------------------

AT SELECTION-SCREEN.
  DATA(lv_fc) = sscrfields-ucomm. " function code

  CASE lv_fc.
    WHEN 'FC01'. " Save Variant
      PERFORM save_variant.

    WHEN 'FC02'. " Load Variant (choose from catalog)
      PERFORM choose_and_apply_variant.

    WHEN 'FC03'. " Delete Variant
      PERFORM choose_and_delete_variant.
  ENDCASE.

  " --- Main logic: simple select for demo ------------------------------------

START-OF-SELECTION.
  SELECT * FROM sflight
    WHERE carrid = @p_carrid
      AND ( connid = @p_connid OR @p_connid IS INITIAL )
  AND ( fldate = @p_fldate OR @p_fldate IS INITIAL )
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_flights).

  IF lt_flights IS INITIAL.
    MESSAGE 'No data found' TYPE 'I'.
  ELSE.
    cl_demo_output=>display( lt_flights ).
  ENDIF.

  " =============================================================================
  " Subroutines
  " =============================================================================
FORM save_variant.
  " 1) Basic checks
  IF p_vname IS INITIAL.
    MESSAGE 'Please enter a variant name in P_VNAME' TYPE 'E'.
  ENDIF.

  " 2) Capture selection-screen values into lt_params
  CLEAR lt_params.
  CALL FUNCTION 'RS_REFRESH_FROM_SELECTOPTIONS'
    EXPORTING
      curr_report     = gv_repid
    TABLES
      selection_table = lt_params
    EXCEPTIONS
      OTHERS          = 1.
  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    MESSAGE 'Unable to read selection screen values' TYPE 'E'.
  ENDIF.

  " 3) Debug: if lt_params is empty, show message and dump content
  IF lt_params IS INITIAL.
    MESSAGE 'No selection values captured (lt_params is empty).' TYPE 'E'.
  ENDIF.

  " Optional: display captured param names/values for debugging
  LOOP AT lt_params INTO DATA(ls_p).
    WRITE: / ls_p-selname, ls_p-kind, ls_p-sign, ls_p-option, ls_p-low, ls_p-high.
  ENDLOOP.

  CLEAR : lt_params, ls_p.

  " description
  lv_var_des-variant = gv_variant.
  lv_var_des-report  = gv_repid.

  " 4) Create variant (user-specific 'U' is recommended)
  gv_variant = p_vname.

  CALL FUNCTION 'RS_CREATE_VARIANT'
    EXPORTING
      curr_report               = gv_repid
      curr_variant              = gv_variant
      vari_desc                 = lv_var_des
      suppress_authority_check  = 'X'
    TABLES
      vari_contents             = lt_params
      vari_text                 = lt_text
    EXCEPTIONS
      illegal_report_or_variant = 1
      illegal_variantname       = 2
      not_authorized            = 3
      not_executed              = 4
      report_not_existent       = 5
      report_not_supplied       = 6
      variant_exists            = 7
      variant_locked            = 8
      OTHERS                    = 9.

  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    " Some systems may need a DB_COMMIT to persist the variant to DB
    COMMIT WORK.
    MESSAGE |Variant { gv_variant } created successfully| TYPE 'S'.
  ELSE.
    MESSAGE 'RS_CREATE_VARIANT failed (sy-subrc <> 0). Please check authorizations and input.' TYPE 'E'.
  ENDIF.
ENDFORM.

FORM choose_and_apply_variant.
  " Choose a variant from catalog (pop-up)
  CALL FUNCTION 'RS_VARIANT_CATALOG'
    EXPORTING
      report      = gv_repid
    IMPORTING
      sel_variant = gv_variant
    EXCEPTIONS
      no_report   = 1
      OTHERS      = 2.

  IF gv_variant IS INITIAL.
    MESSAGE 'No variant selected.' TYPE 'I'.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  " Apply variant values to the selection screen
  CALL FUNCTION 'RS_SUPPORT_SELECTIONS'
    EXPORTING
      report               = gv_repid
      variant              = gv_variant
    EXCEPTIONS
      variant_not_existent = 1
      variant_obsolete     = 2
      OTHERS               = 3.

  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    MESSAGE |Variant { gv_variant } applied to screen. Press Execute.| TYPE 'S'.
  ELSE.
    MESSAGE 'Failed to apply variant' TYPE 'E'.
  ENDIF.
ENDFORM.

FORM choose_and_delete_variant.
  " Choose a variant from catalog to delete
  CALL FUNCTION 'RS_VARIANT_CATALOG'
    EXPORTING
      report      = gv_repid
    IMPORTING
      sel_variant = gv_variant
    EXCEPTIONS
      no_report   = 1
      OTHERS      = 2.

  IF gv_variant IS INITIAL.
    MESSAGE 'No variant selected.' TYPE 'I'.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  " Delete variant
  CALL FUNCTION 'RS_VARIANT_DELETE'
    EXPORTING
      report  = gv_repid
      variant = gv_variant
    EXCEPTIONS
      OTHERS  = 1.

  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    COMMIT WORK.
    MESSAGE |Variant { gv_variant } deleted successfully| TYPE 'S'.
  ELSE.
    MESSAGE 'Unable to delete variant' TYPE 'E'.
  ENDIF.
ENDFORM.

 Execute our program--

Give the variant details and click on save--

Now click on load variant--

Now we can delete any variant-- 

Click on delete variant->select anyone variant->click Ok, it will delete.

This allows the user to enter values and manage variants dynamically.

Function Key Labels.

Variant Lifecycle Flow-- 

Here’s the process our program demonstrates:-

[Save Variant] ---> [Load Variant] ---> [Apply Variant] ---> [Delete Variant]
  • Save → Captures current selection values and stores them as a variant. 
  • Load → Lets you choose an existing variant from a catalog. 
  • Apply → Populates the selection screen with saved values. 
  • Delete → Removes an existing variant. 

Handling Each Operation-- 

Save Variant-- 

  • Capture screen values with RS_REFRESH_FROM_SELECTOPTIONS. 
  • Call RS_CREATE_VARIANT with these values. 
  • Commit to DB with COMMIT WORK.

Note - Common issue: sy-subrc = 1 -- check variant name and ensure vari_desc - report = sy-  repid.

Load & Apply Variant-- 

  • Use RS_VARIANT_CATALOG to show a pop-up of available variants. 
  • Pass the chosen variant to RS_SUPPORT_SELECTIONS to populate the screen.

Delete Variant-- 

  • Again, call RS_VARIANT_CATALOG to pick a variant. 
  • Use RS_VARIANT_DELETE to remove it. 
  • Always commit the change (COMMIT WORK)

Conclusion-- 

Programmatically handling variants in ABAP gives developers flexibility to: 

  • Automate repetitive report executions. 
  • Provide ready-to-use variants to end users. 
  • Dynamically manage selection screens in complex business processes. 

By mastering RS_CREATE_VARIANT, RS_VARIANT_CATALOG, and related function modules, you can fully control the variant lifecycle from ABAP. 

Thanks you..

entry.title: Re: S4 HANA 2020 Upgrade On Premise - S4 HANA 2023 or S4...
entry.content:
1) 2020 → 2023

  • This is a shorter jump with fewer release differences.
  • Upgrade effort (testing, custom code adaptation, regression checks) will be less intensive compared to skipping multiple versions.
  • Lower risk of incompatibility issues since you’re only bridging three years of innovations.

2) 2020 → 2025

  • This is a larger leap across multiple releases.
  • More functional changes, simplifications, and innovations to absorb at once.
  • Testing and adaptation will be more time-consuming, especially for custom developments and integrations.
  • However, it avoids doing two upgrades in quick succession.

entry.title: Re: Total Compensation Plan Template
entry.content:
Yes for a Total Compensation Template you must have one hierarchy across both compensation and variable pay.  HBA is not supported for Total Comp so you must select one of the other available hierarchies to use this functionality.

Supported Features of Total Compensation Templates | SAP Help Portal

Hope that helps,

Christian

entry.title: Re: Batch creation on material to material stock posting
entry.content: Automatic batch creation is not supported out-of-the-box for movement type 309. The system expects a batch to be provided because it needs to maintain traceability and consistency in batch management. If you want to achieve this requirement, it requires custom development (BAdI/User Exit + batch creation logic) but decide whether automatic batch creation fits your compliance and traceability requirements before implementing.

entry.title: Re: Service Entry Sheet release strategy should trigger f...
entry.content:
This is a classic challenge with Service Entry Sheet (SES) release strategies in SAP. The net value field (CESSR-LWERT) is stored with a currency data type, which complicates using it directly as a numeric characteristic in the release strategy.

Why this happens is because, CESSR-LWERT is defined as a currency field in SAP. When you create a characteristic in CT04, the system automatically defaults the data type to "Currency format" because of the underlying field definition. Release strategy characteristics cannot directly convert currency fields into pure numeric values and they retain the currency formatting.

What you can do is keep the characteristic as currency type. Define your release strategy condition as “greater than 0”; this works across currencies, since the system evaluates the numeric part regardless of currency. The limitation is that you cannot strip the currency out, but for your requirement (“any SES with net value > 0”), this is usually sufficient. The other option is if your business process always requires release for SES with value > 0, you could simplify by using a document type–based release strategy combined with a value check in a user exit. This avoids the complexity of currency formatting altogether.

entry.title: Scheduling of background jobs in production system - Access related query
entry.content:
Hi,

I have a question regarding access-related concerns in the production system.

We have developed a few background jobs in the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and successfully tested them. These jobs have now been transported to the production system. Currently, the background jobs are scheduled in production by our functional team.

However, since the production system is meant for business use, our access will be revoked once the project is completed, as we are not part of the customer’s organization. In this context, I would like some clarification on the following points:

  1. After our access is revoked, will the already scheduled background jobs be cancelled automatically? If so, will the business users need to reschedule them?

  2. If the background job continues to run and creates subsequent documents after our access is revoked, will those documents remain valid, or will they be discarded?

If there is a risk in the current approach, could you please advise on how this can be handled correctly? Specifically, what is the recommended best practice for scheduling and managing background jobs in the production system?

entry.title: Re: Avoid create sales order from certain sales office an...
entry.content:
To meet the requirement of restricting sales order and billing creation by Sales Office and Business Area, the only feasible option is to use a user exit/BAdI since SAP standard authorization objects (V_VBAK_VKO, V_VBRK_VKO) do not check these fields.

Instead of hard‑coding the logic inside the exit, I recommend maintaining the allowed combinations in the TVARVC table (Sales Area ↔ Sales Office, and Sales Area ↔ Business Area). During sales order creation (VA01) and billing (VF01/VF04), the relevant user exits can read these entries from TVARVC and validate whether the user is authorized. If the combination is not permitted, the system will restrict the transaction accordingly.

This approach ensures:

  • Flexibility: Business can adjust combinations in TVARVC without code changes.
  • Maintainability: No hard‑coded values in ABAP, reducing long‑term support effort.
  • Scalability: Same logic can be extended to other authorization checks if needed.

In summary, while SAP does not provide a standard authorization object for Sales Office or Business Area, implementing the check via user exits with TVARVC‑based configuration is the recommended and sustainable solution.

entry.title: Re: Exchnage rates
entry.content:
Good day @jerrysam 

Thank you for your question.

For electronic export invoices, when the system calculates the taxes to be shown in the XML, it uses the tax calculation date from the source document.

So, if your export scenario is using the shipping or goods issue related date as the tax calculation date in the source flow, then the tax related exchange rate used for the XML and eDocument process can follow that date, not the billing date. That is why you can see the system behaving as if it is taking the rate from shipping instead of billing for the KSeF relevant rules.

That also explains your second symptom. So, yes, it is possible to see one exchange rate effect for the net value and another for VAT. The likely reason is that the commercial or pricing amounts in your billing document follow the billing document and pricing logic, while the tax amounts shown in your XML or eDocument follow the tax calculation date from the source document.

Question: what can be done to consider the exchange rate for billing document especially for export goods as that can correct e document cockpit to submit to ksef.

Answer: In Public Cloud, the first standard thing you should check is not eDocument Cockpit. The first thing to check is which date is being used as the tax calculation date in your source sales process. If the date is derived from shipping or goods issue for export goods, then eDocument processing will follow that logic for the tax values it sends to the XML. In that case, changing only the billing document date will not correct the KSeF submission values itself.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/9e68f95f727744cf915ee5b38e792aa8/6191425fb653453f8ff2c61202f1c543.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/9e68f95f727744cf915ee5b38e792aa8/f86a51ccfb5b4611ae6c88419123e647.html?locale=en-US

Creating Sales Billing Documents in Contract Accounting | SAP Help Portal

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Zinc Alloy (ZAMAC) for Basic Material Fraction in RDP
entry.content:
It seems that Zinc-alloy is not frequently used in packaging, except for the cosmetics and luxury spirits industries, where it is used for closures or decorative elements. Perhaps that's the reason why no customer has brought it up yet.

The other reason why this chemical compound doesn't exist in the list of basic fractions is that PROs do not require separate reporting for it.

In reports that have "Other non-ferrous metals", zinc-alloy will be reported in this category.

In UK EPR, which recognizes only Aluminum and Steel in the Metals family, zinc-alloy will be reported in "Other"

The customer is free to create an influence request and we'll need to figure it out.

The closest basic fractions today are "aluminum" and non-magnetic steel, because they are non-ferrous

entry.title: A Missing Distinction in Master Data: Why SAP S/4HANA Cloud Needs a Dedicated Business Partner Role
entry.content:
A Missing Distinction in Master Data:

Why SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Needs a Dedicated Business Partner Role for Manufacturers

Master data clarity is a cornerstone of efficient procurement and material management processes in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. Business Partners (BP) play a central role in ensuring that responsibilities, relationships, and business contexts are clearly modeled in the system.

While SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud already provides a Business Partner role for Suppliers, an important real‑world distinction is currently missing: Manufacturers are treated like any other Business Partner, despite playing a fundamentally different role in the business process.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

The Business Reality: Suppliers Are Not Manufacturers

In typical procurement scenarios:

  • Suppliers are contractual partners
    → We place purchase orders, receive invoices, and process payments with them.

  • Manufacturers produce the goods
    → There is no direct commercial relationship with them.

We buy materials from suppliers, not from manufacturers — yet manufacturers are essential for:

  • Product identification
  • Compliance and regulatory requirements
  • Quality management
  • Supplier‑independent material transparency

This distinction is common business practice, but currently not reflected cleanly in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud master data.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

The Gap Today: No Dedicated BP Role for Manufacturers

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  • Business Partners can be created
  • BP roles such as Supplier can be assigned
  • However, no BP role exists for “Manufacturer”

This leads to a concrete issue in daily master data maintenance.

Example: Material Master Data (Purchasing View)

To maintain the Manufacturer field (MFRNR) in the Material Master (Purchasing view), the system requires:

  • Selection of a Business Partner

Since there is no BP role specifically for manufacturers:

  • Manufacturers must be created using unrelated or generic BP roles
  • Sometimes even as suppliers — despite no purchasing relationship

The result: inconsistent and misleading master data.

 

 

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Impact of the Current Situation

The missing BP role for manufacturers causes multiple challenges:

  • Data inconsistency Manufacturers appear in the system without a clearly defined business context.

  • Blurring of responsibilities Suppliers and manufacturers cannot be cleanly distinguished in analytics, governance, or data ownership.

  • Reduced data quality Master data no longer reflects the real business relationship.

  • Higher risk in compliance and audits Especially when manufacturer information is legally or regulatorily relevant.

Instead of supporting clean master data modeling, the system forces customers into workarounds.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

The Opportunity: Introduce a Dedicated BP Role “Manufacturer”

The proposed improvement is conceptually simple but highly valuable:

Introduce a dedicated Business Partner role for “Manufacturer”.

This would allow customers to:

  • Clearly separate manufacturers from suppliers
  • Assign manufacturers only where they are actually required (e.g. Material Master)
  • Prevent incorrect role usage and misinterpretation

Most importantly, it aligns SAP master data modeling with real‑world business structures.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Business Benefits of This Improvement

Introducing a BP role for manufacturers enables:

  • Clearer master data structures
    Distinct, purpose‑driven Business Partner roles.

  • Improved data quality and consistency
    Manufacturer information is stored correctly and transparently.

  • Better governance and compliance support
    Especially relevant in regulated industries.

  • Improved user experience
    Master data specialists no longer need to apply workarounds or compromises.

  • Future‑proof data models
    Clean separation allows better extensibility and reporting.

________________________________________________________________________________________________________

Call to Action: Help Improve SAP Master Data Modeling

This improvement request is based on real customer requirements and widely used procurement processes. If you face similar challenges or agree that manufacturers deserve their own Business Partner role, I encourage you to:

👉Please Vote to Idea # 353179 on SAP Influence and help move this enhancement forward.

entry.title: Re: Scheduling of background jobs in production system -...
entry.content:
Hello @Thiru2203 

Background jobs already scheduled in production will continue to run even after project user access is revoked. No rescheduling is required.
Any documents created by these jobs after access revocation remain valid and are not impacted from a business or audit perspective.

Risk & Recommendation:

To avoid dependency on temporary project users, SAP best practice is to assign background jobs to a dedicated, business-owned technical user with appropriate authorizations. Ownership for monitoring and maintenance should reside with the business team.


Best regards,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Scheduling of background jobs in production system -...
entry.content:
Once background jobs are scheduled in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, they are owned by the system rather than by the individual user who created them.

1) Scheduled jobs after access revocation:
The jobs will continue to run as scheduled even after your project access is revoked. They are not automatically cancelled when a user’s access is removed, because the scheduling is stored in the system job repository.

2) Subsequent documents created by jobs:
Any documents generated by these jobs will remain valid business documents. They are not discarded or invalidated simply because the original scheduler no longer has access. The system executes them under its own background processing framework.

3) Risk and best practice:
The risk lies in jobs being tied to project users rather than business ownership. Best practice is to:

  • ensure jobs are scheduled under a technical/system user or a business-owned user ID that will remain active after go-live.
  • document the purpose, frequency, and ownership of each job so the customer’s support team can manage them.
  • transfer responsibility for monitoring and rescheduling jobs to the business IT team before project closure.
  • avoid using temporary project accounts for production scheduling.

entry.title: Re: Custom Container issue
entry.content:
Hello @uraniym 

Observed an issue with nested ALV popups where previously displayed records reappear, even though the underlying data is refreshed correctly.
Root Cause:
When using SALV with custom containers, the ALV binds to the data only during first creation. Reusing the same SALV instance or container causes cached data to be displayed, even after the internal table is updated.

Recommendation:
For reliable behavior, each popup (and nested popup) should:

Create a new custom container
Create a new SALV instance
Explicitly free both upon popup closure

This aligns with SALV’s design limitations. If frequent data refresh within the same container is required, switching to ALV Grid is recommended.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Dynamic Handling of Variants in ABAP – Save, Load, Delete
entry.content:
Thanks. Many other blog posts and discussions exist: https://www.google.com/search?q=%22rs_create_variant%22+blog+post%20site:sap.com

There are many known challenges and limits, like parameter values with more than 45 characters -> RS_CREATE_VARIANT_255. Parameters of any type and any length -> to be defined (maybe writing the global variables of parameters of programs via dirty assign + calling RS_VARIANT_VALUE_SAVE and RS_CREATE_VARIANT if creation is needed). Dynamic Selections -> to be defined (probably via RS_VARIANT_VALUE_SAVE). ETC.

Other "API" (unofficial): RS_CHANGE_CREATED_VARIANT to change an existing program variant.

Official API: BAPI_XBP_VARIANT_CREATE, BAPI_XBP_VARIANT_CHANGE, BAPI_XBP_VARIANT_COPY, BAPI_XBP_VARIANT_DELETE, etc. NB: they need also calling BAPI_XMI_* function modules.

entry.title: Driving the Future of Energy Flexibility with SAP Distributed Energy Resources
entry.content:
Dear Utilities Community,

I’m excited to share key insights into our new offering within SAP Distributed Energy Resources.

Built on SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Distributed Energy Resources (SAP DER) provides a trusted data backbone that unifies technical assets with commercial agreements, transforming millions of prosumer devices into coordinated services. From energy sharing and dynamic pricing to smart charging and flexibility markets, cloud native and interoperable, it connects meters, markets, and customers to enable scenario driven business models such as energy communities, virtual power plants, and demand response. Combined with a curated ecosystem of specialist technology partners, SAP delivers a comprehensive end to end offering that empowers utilities to own their energy transition. 

The energy transition runs on data, but data are getting harder to manage 

The energy transition is reshaping the power landscape. Utilities are evolving from centralized generation toward systems powered by millions of distributed energy resources (DERs): solar panels, EV chargers, heat pumps, and batteries owned by consumers and prosumers alike. 

This transformation brings extraordinary potential, and unprecedented complexity. The number of connected assets and actors is growing rapidly, generating vast amounts of operational, commercial, and customer data. Managing these data reliably and consistently is one of the defining challenges of the next energy decade. 

At the same time, utilities face a mounting competitive threat. Digital energy platforms and new market entrants are intermediating the customer relationship, offering integrated propositions that bundle connectivity, flexibility, and billing into a single stack. Utilities that do not act risk being reduced to commodity providers while others capture the value of the energy transition. 

To fully unlock the value of distributed energy resources, utilities need more than connectivity. They need data orchestration: a trusted, intelligent foundation that harmonizes information across every process, partner, and device, and that ensures they own rather than rent their energy transition capabilities. 

From distributed assets to orchestrated intelligence 

SAP’s SAP Distributed Energy Resources (SAP DER) solution is designed to meet exactly this challenge. Built on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), it enables utilities to manage DER related data at scale, across production, consumption, and storage, while ensuring reliability, performance, and consistency.

Rather than acting as an operational control system, the SAP DER solution focuses on data orchestration and integration. It ensures that DER data can flow seamlessly between operational, commercial, and analytical systems, providing a single source of truth that connects the physical and digital worlds of energy. 

“The complexity our customers face with prosumer data is exponential. They need reliability, performance, and consistency across all their data sources, and that’s exactly where SAP Distributed Energy Resources comes in.” 

— Kim Maren Ekrutt, Global Industry Business Unit Utilities Co‑Head, SAP 

Two pillars of the SAP DER solution 

1. Complex Installation and Prosumer Data Orchestration (Technical and Commercial Foundation) 

Manage the full spectrum of prosumer related data, covering both complex technical installation details, such as meters, solar panels, batteries, EV chargers, and heat pumps, and the commercial information required to operate distributed energy resources at scale. This includes customer and prosumer master data, product structures, tariffs, contracts, grid connection agreements, feed in contracts, and all commercial relationships linked to each installation. 

By bringing together technical asset data with commercial attributes and agreement information, the solution provides a unified and reliable model of every installation. This is essential because distributed energy resources cannot be managed with technical information alone. Commercial products, grid contracts, flexible tariffs, and incentive agreements determine how energy flows are measured, settled, billed, shared, or monetized. 

This enhanced pillar becomes the foundation for energy communities, virtual power plant participation, flexibility programs, dynamic pricing, capacity and congestion management, and any business model involving prosumers and distributed energy assets. 

2. Energy Management and Sharing 

Track and analyze how energy is consumed, produced, stored, and shared, enabling new energy community and peer-to-peer business models. With high resolution energy data and intelligent allocation algorithms, utilities can offer transparent, fair, and regulation compliant energy sharing services to residential and commercial participants alike. 

Connecting data, processes, and people 

SAP’s strength lies in its ability to bridge operational and customer domains. Utilities already rely on SAP for mission critical processes such as meter to cash, asset management, and customer experience. SAP DER extends this foundation, linking every asset, agreement, transaction, and customer interaction within one coherent data model. 

This integrated approach turns technical data into business value, powering use cases such as smart tariffs, dynamic pricing, and energy sharing, while ensuring that customer experience and grid efficiency evolve together. For utilities, this means a single enterprise backbone that connects the traditional meter to cash world with the emerging flexibility and DER economy. 

From SAP Cloud for Energy to the next generation SAP Distributed Energy Resources, energy data management component 

The SAP DER solution represents the natural evolution of SAP's Cloud for Energy capabilities. At its core is a powerful energy data management engine designed to handle the full lifecycle of energy data at scale. It supports real time energy data ingestion from smart meters and IoT devices, comprehensive validations, estimations, manual editing, and anomaly detection, ensuring that every data point is reliable and audit ready. 

The platform serves as the central repository for energy data across the utility, providing the trusted backbone for analytics, energy forecasting, and advanced reporting. It can store interval data across multiple dimensions and accommodates diverse data types beyond energy and demand, including pricing data, weather, and other non-energy time series. This multidimensional capability enables utilities to correlate energy flows with market prices, weather patterns, and demand signals within a single coherent data model. 

Energy sharing and energy aggregation capabilities allow utilities to allocate, redistribute, and aggregate energy flows across prosumers, energy communities, and portfolios, supporting the commercial and regulatory requirements of emerging flexibility business models. 

Built on the public cloud, the energy data management component of the SAP DER solution delivers greater flexibility, scalability, and extensibility, offering a modular structure where utilities can adopt only those capabilities they need, from basic energy data management to full DER orchestration. This evolution supports SAP's broader strategy of modernizing its utilities portfolio towards cloud native, interoperable, and data driven operations, providing utilities with a future proof platform that grows alongside their ambitions. 

A partner ecosystem for the energy flexibility market 

No single vendor can address the full breadth of the distributed energy landscape. That is why SAP has assembled a curated ecosystem of specialist technology partners, each bringing deep domain expertise that complements SAP’s enterprise data platform. Together, SAP and its partners deliver a comprehensive, end-to-end offering that covers the complete spectrum of emerging flexibility and DER business models. 

This ecosystem approach is built on a fundamental principle: utilities should own their energy transition capabilities, not rent them. Rather than ceding control to monolithic platforms that intermediate the customer relationship, SAP enables utilities to build on their existing enterprise backbone while plugging in best of breed partner capabilities through open, cloud native integrations. Data stays with the utility. Business logic remains under their control. And the customer relationship belongs to them. 

Specialist capabilities across the flexibility value chain 

SAP Distributed Energy Resources: Enabling the Energy Flexibility Market. SAP’s technology partners extend the platform across six critical domains of the energy flexibility market: 

  • Flexibility Management and Virtual Power Plants: Connect, orchestrate, and monetize prosumer assets including EVs, batteries, heat pumps, and solar inverters. Enable utilities to participate in flexibility markets, build virtual power plants, and deliver smart charging and home energy management services. 
  • Energy Communities and Energy Sharing: Design, manage, and operate energy communities with transparent allocation, dynamic local pricing, and full regulatory compliance. Enable self-consumption optimization and structured power purchasing agreements for commercial and residential participants. 
  • Grid Congestion and Capacity Management: Forecast, detect, and resolve grid congestion at the low voltage network level. Optimize capacity allocation and flexibility dispatch to maintain grid stability while unlocking flexibility revenues for distribution system operators and connected utilities. 
  • Energy Portfolio Management and Trading: Access wholesale and flexibility markets with sophisticated portfolio management, real time pricing, and complex B2B tariff structures. Enable spot and index-based billing, dynamic quotations, and risk management for utilities expanding into energy trading. 
  • DER Customer Insights and Energy Disaggregation: Deliver behind the meter visibility through non-intrusive load monitoring, EV detection, and appliance level analytics. Transform smart meter data into actionable customer insights that drive engagement, targeted offerings, and proactive grid planning. 
  • Demand Response and Load Management: Design and execute demand response programs that align consumer behavior with grid and market signals. Combine customer incentives, dynamic pricing, and automated device control to shape demand profiles and reduce peak loads. 

Each partner integration is integrated using SAP BTP, ensuring enterprise grade security, scalability, and seamless data exchange with SAP’s core utilities processes. This means that flexibility and DER capabilities are not bolted on as separate silos but woven into the fabric of the utility’s operational and commercial systems. 

Co‑innovation with utilities, for utilities 

SAP is co-innovating with major utilities across Europe, North America, and Asia Pacific to ensure that the SAP DER solution and its partner ecosystem reflect real operational needs, from managing data complexity to supporting new energy market dynamics. 

Together, this ecosystem addresses the full spectrum of emerging business models: 

  • Energy communities and virtual power plants 
  • Demand response, dynamic pricing, and demand side management 
  • Grid congestion management and capacity optimization 
  • Load shaping and flexibility management 
  • Energy portfolio management and trading 

DER customer insights, home energy management, and energy disaggregation 

This collaborative approach enables utilities to combine SAP’s trusted data backbone with specialized capabilities from the wider energy technology ecosystem, accelerating innovation while ensuring reliability, compliance, and full control of the customer relationship. 

“Our role is to help utilities master the growing data complexity and turn it into opportunity. With SAP Distributed Energy Resources and our partner ecosystem, we’re connecting data, processes, and partners into one intelligent platform that enables utilities to own their energy transition.” 

— Kim Maren Ekrutt, Global Industry Business Unit Utilities Co‑Head, SAP 


Join SAP and our DER partners at the SAP for Energy and Utilities Conference in Toulouse from April 21–23—a great opportunity to connect, learn, and exchange ideas at our dedicated DER booth. Check out my post for more details and register now.

entry.title: Re: Recurring supplier invoice: Fill in mandatory field I...
entry.content:
Good day @FabianSCC 

Thank you for your question.

The error you are getting highlights that the execution of the recurring supplier invoice is receiving incomplete item level data for 3 G/L related rows, specifically a missing INVOICE_DOC_ITEM in GLACCOUNTDATA. In practice, this is usually caused by a template content issue in those rows. As a next step, I suggest you download a fresh Recurring Supplier Invoice Template (not a copied old file), compare the failing rows against a working row at field level, and rebuild (the other file might be corrupted already) those 3 rows in a clean file.

Here is how I suggest you go about it:

  • Open the recurring Supplier Invoice template again and compare the 3 failing rows against a working row using the raw downloaded template, not a copied old file.
  • Check whether the 3 rows have a missing or inconsistent item number/invoice document item value in the item level data.
  • Check whether those rows contain a different account assignment combination, tax setup, withholding tax data, or split logic that caused the system to generate a separate GLACCOUNTDATA entry.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/7b0ac5d9bcd142279caec96c52510552.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/32cfbcdebe364418820ccb240491b59b.html?locale=en-US

  • Recreate the template from scratch in the app,

enter just 2 or 3 lines manually, and then add the problematic lines one by one. This helps you isolate which row starts generating the GLACCOUNTDATA error.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Which Integration Scenario Are You In? 3 SAP BTP Guides to Help You Move Forward
entry.content:
As organizations accelerate their cloud transformation journeys, integration remains a critical foundation for connecting systems, processes, and data across landscapes.

Within the SAP BTP Value Map, we have published a series of knowledge-based articles to support you in different integration scenarios — whether you are building new capabilities, modernizing existing landscapes, or planning your migration to the cloud.

Below are three key scenarios you might be facing - and how you can get started.

 

🔹Scenario 1: Building Integration Expertise on SAP BTP

If you are an integration consultant or enterprise architect looking to deepen your expertise in SAP’s integration strategy and technologies…

Or if you want to better understand:

  • Core integration capabilities
  • API Management and Event Integration
  • Integration architecture and best practices

👉You may find this guide highly valuable:
SAP Integration Suite Black Belt Empowerment Guide

This enablement program is designed to help you become a subject matter expert in SAP Integration Suite, providing structured learning content, best practices, and practical insights to support your integration journey.

 

🔹Scenario 2: Migrating from SAP Neo to Multi-Cloud (Cloud Foundry)

If you are currently running integration scenarios in the SAP BTP Neo environment and are considering your move to the multi-cloud foundation…

Or if you are asking:

  • What is the right path forward from Neo?
  • How do I migrate my existing integration content?
  • What changes should I expect in Cloud Foundry?

👉This guide walks you through the process step by step:
SAP Neo, Cloud Integration to SAP Integration Suite Migration Guide

It provides practical insights, screenshots, and structured guidance to help you transition your integration scenarios from Neo to SAP Integration Suite on Cloud Foundry.

 

🔹Scenario 3: Transitioning from SAP PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite

If your organization is planning to move away from SAP Process Integration / Process Orchestration (PI/PO)…

Or if you are exploring:

  • Why SAP Integration Suite is the strategic direction
  • How to approach the migration
  • What support services are available

👉You can refer to this article:
How to Migrate from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite through Support Services

This guide outlines the migration approach based on SAP Activate methodology, helping you understand key phases, available options, and how SAP services can support your transition.

 

🚀Start Your Integration Journey with Confidence

Whether you are building new integration capabilities or modernizing your existing landscape, the SAP BTP Value Map provides structured guidance, curated content, and expert insights to support you every step of the way.

👉Explore these articles in the SAP BTP Value Map and take the next step in your integration journey.

We look forward to your feedback and engagement in the community.

entry.title: AI Comes to the Classroom: Updated Academic Lesson on SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
A Lesson That Keeps Pace with the Technology

If you have used the Academic Lesson on "Designing Stories with SAP Analytics Cloud" before, you already know it as a solid bridge between textbook concepts and real-world analytics tooling. The lesson walks students through data exploration in SAP Analytics Cloud, then into the design of impactful data stories, all with free access to SAP Learning Hub, student edition courses and practice systems.

The latest release, **24-sac-001d**, builds on that foundation with a meaningful addition: Activity 3: Applying AI-Powered Visualizations and Augmented Analytics to Business Data in SAP Analytics Cloud.

What Is New in This Update
A Third Activity Focused on AI and Augmented Analytics

The previous version (24-sac-002c, August 2025) included two activities:

Activity 1 - Exploring SAP Analytics Cloud (navigation, modeling, planning features)
Activity 2 - Designing Stories in SAP Analytics Cloud (building and presenting data-driven Stories)

This update adds:

Activity 3 - Applying AI-Powered Visualizations and Augmented Analytics to Business Data in SAP Analytics Cloud

Activity 3 draws from the SAP Learning Hub, student edition course of the same name and introduces students to two units of content:

1. Introducing AI, Machine Learning, and Augmented Analytics - foundational concepts on how AI and machine learning underpin modern business intelligence.
2. Using SAP Analytics Cloud Smart Features - hands-on work with Just Ask, the natural-language query feature that lets users type a question and receive an auto-generated visualization, and Smart Insights, which surfaces data patterns automatically.

Students complete the two units (approximately 52 minutes of course content), earn passing scores on the associated quizzes, and work through a practice exercise where they build a Just Ask Canvas Story live in SAP Analytics Cloud.

New Discussion Questions That Go Beyond the Tool

Activity 3 also comes with three discussion questions designed to engage student critical thinking:

  1. Responsible use of AI-generated visualizations: Students consider what happens when a manager asks them to drop a Just Ask chart into a quarterly business review. Who is responsible for accuracy? What should be verified before presenting AI-generated output?
  2. Data quality and forecasting: Students examine how flawed input data affects forecast reliability, connecting back to the "Garbage In, Garbage Out" principle from Activity 2.
  3. Audience-driven design with AI content: Students compare how a 12-month sales forecast story should be designed differently for an internal sales team versus executive leadership, including how to handle confidence intervals and the visual distinction between actuals and predictions.

These questions are ready to use as class discussions, group activities, essay prompts, or inter-teaching exercises. Suggested answers are included for instructors as a starting point of discussion.

Why This Update Matters for Your Classroom

The shift from specialist-driven analytics to business-user-driven analytics is one of the most significant trends in the data landscape. Organizations increasingly expect graduates, regardless of discipline, to be comfortable interpreting data and questioning AI-generated outputs. 

Here is what makes it practical for teaching

Low barrier to entry - Activity 3 requires only about 52 minutes of course content and one practice exercise. You can add it to an existing assignment without overhauling your syllabus.

No prerequisites beyond the lesson itself -  Students who complete Activities 1 and 2 have all the context they need. The lesson remains accessible to undergraduate and graduate students across business, information systems, social sciences, natural sciences, engineering, medicine, journalism, and beyond.

Free access - The SAP Learning Hub student edition, the courses, and the practice systems are all available at no cost to students and lecturers at institutions of higher education.

Real AI, not simulated -  Students interact with actual AI-powered features in SAP Analytics Cloud. They type natural-language questions, receive generated charts, and evaluate the results, it is hands-on experience with enterprise-grade augmented analytics.

Critical thinking built in - The discussion questions encourage students to treat AI as a starting point, not a finished product. That mindset, evaluating, refining, and contextualizing AI output is one of the most important skill students can develop right now.

How to Get Started

The updated lesson is available for download in two formats:

PDF Version: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/u6XbMD1?rc=10&doi=SAP1200446](https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/u6XbMD1?rc=10&doi=SAP1200446
Word Version: https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/keWdTC4?rc=10&doi=SAP1191525](https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/keWdTC4?rc=10&doi=SAP1191525

The Word version is fully editable, so you can adapt the student instructions, deliverables, and discussion questions to fit your course design. Add requirements, remove what does not apply, and make it your own.

To access the courses and practice systems, students and lecturers will need an SAP Universal ID and enrollment in the SAP Learning Hub, student edition. If this is your first time using materials as part of the SAP University Alliances program, visit the Getting Started resources linked within the lesson document for setup guidance.

A Note on Flexibility

One of the strengths of this Academic Lesson has always been its adaptability. You do not have to assign all three Activities. You can assign any combination that fits your learning outcomes. You can use the discussion questions as graded essays, informal class discussions, or skip them entirely.

That said, if you are looking for one update to bring into your next offering, Activity 3 is a strong candidate. Giving students hands-on time with AI-augmented analytics, alongside the critical thinking prompts to evaluate what the AI produces, is a combination that serves them well regardless of where their careers take them.

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud TRM-Special G/L indicator not define...
entry.content:
Good day @espani 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, you do not resolve what you described by changing posting keys with OB41 or similar classic config. Instead, the correction point is the TRM account determination setup. KBA 3719928 explains that scenario.

That error means that the posting generated by the TRM reversal is trying to use a posting key plus account type plus G/L indicator combination that is not valid. As mentioned in KBA 3719928, you must ensure the special G/L indicator used in the TRM posting specification is valid for the account type assigned to the posting key. Also, verify that account symbols such as V.0 are assigned only to vendors, and C.0 only to customers.

So, configuration inconsistencies in TRM account determination are the root cause and not usually the loan reversal app itself. These could be:

  • The posting specification uses a special G/L indicator that does not fit the posting key
  • The posting specification points to the wrong account symbol/account type
  • The same posting specification is reused across scenarios where it should be split, for example paying versus non paying valuation areas.

Your comparison with on premise is understandable, the Public Cloud supported path is not for you to change the posting key definition. The KBA mentioned earlier points to the configuration content, not to OB41.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/e5ec5859d8e54df98492d80564a734c0/8e0dda531198434de10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

 

The next step is for you to reproduce the reversal in Process Business Transactions,

identify the affected update type and posting specification and then review whether the posting spec is incorrectly using a customer or vendor special G/L setup, or whether the same posting spec is incorrectly shared across paying and non-paying valuation area scenarios.

 

To summarise:

  • No, in Public Cloud you should not solve your scenario by changing posting key customizing like OB41
  • Yes, the issue is most likely a TRM account determination/Posting specification case
  • The correct place for you to fix the issue is SSCUI 103014.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: 客户项目首付款开票流程
entry.content:
本博文简单介绍Professional Service客户项目首付款开票流程

系统演示

1.创建客户项目，建立开票，案例用的是固定价格合同开票类型

合同总金额3000，首付款1000

2.APP：管理项目开票，点击黄色三角形查看预付款金额

点击设置按钮，把字段Down Payment Received和Down Payment Requested勾选。

选择开票项，点击Prepayment

点击Submit

开票请求已生成

APP:创建开票凭证，设置开票类型FAZ，对首付款进行开票并过账生成日记账

点击Entry View查看分录

3. APP: Post Incoming Payment

查看日记账分录

4.创建总发票，APP:管理项目开票，点击Prepare Billing

开票凭证请求已生成

APP:创建开票凭证，设置开票类型CI01

查看日记账分录，冲销了首付款的分录

感谢观看

entry.title: Re: How to Segragate Payment terms for two different Comp...
entry.content:
Dear @LALIT_RAH1,

In Public Cloud is no standard option to segregate or hide Terms of Payment by company code in the value help. Payment terms are maintained centrally in SSCUI 102934 Maintain Terms of Payment and SAP confirms that related defaults are driven by the payment term itself, not by company code

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2621708

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3207431

The supported approach is to:

  • create separate payment term keys for each company code with clear naming, and
  • default the correct payment term from the relevant master data or document context
  • Feature request to SAP

More details below:

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/11

Summary:

You can separate the payment term codes but not the value-help list by company code in standard Public Cloud. The practical workaround is controlled naming plus correct defaulting from BP/master data

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: HTML5 (Custom Fiori) app fails with 500 (token exchan...
entry.content:
@ HI cguttikonda24 ,

Thanks for  checking this.
Yes, CAP is bound to the application XSUAA, and the HTML5 Application Repository uses its own SAP‑managed XSUAA. This setup is identical in DEV, TST, and ACC and is the standard SAP pattern. The application works in DEV and ACC with this configuration.

In TST, however, the failure happens in the managed Launchpad runtime while loading static HTML5 resources, before any CAP backend call is reached. We noticed that unlike another working project, this application does not expose the application XSUAA as an instance‑level destination for the HTML5 runtime context. Although DEV and ACC work without it (likely due to existing runtime context).

entry.title: Re: Schedule overhead accounting jobs - select current pe...
entry.content:
Dear @kezelda,

SAP do not documented variable/place-holder that you can enter in Settlement Period / Posting Period / Fiscal Year for Actual Settlement: Projects (SAP) so the job automatically rolls to the current fiscal period/year. SAP documents dynamic behavior only for job templates that explicitly provide dynamic date / dynamic key date handling for example Dynamic Valuation Key Date or dynamic billing date ranges

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/e3f896e4b0af4d1dbf00420e268221b0.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/11742f55d673bc06e10000000a441470.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/b710108518854368b59b0d8212db3f6d.html

For Actual Settlement: Projects, SAP own KBA shows these are treated as explicit parameters. In particular, SAP states that although Posting Period is not marked mandatory, it is still required if Posting Date is entered, which confirms the template expects concrete fiscal-period input rather than an automatic current period/year variable

So the supported practical approach is:

  • schedule the job as recurring
  • but maintain the relevant Settlement Period / Posting Period / Fiscal Year values explicitly
  • or create/update the job variant each month

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3656677

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Add Value Help to SAP Fiori Elements App
entry.content: Tried the similar approach but the error remains unchanged. MPC_EXT did add the value help entity, metadata is showing the same. However, the DPC_EXT enhancement is little confusing. On my standard service, DPC_EXT was not redefined. So the enhancement had to go to the end of GET_ENTITYSET from DPC(which will be wiped off when SAP regenerates the project). Even then, I see the data not being loaded. My understanding from the error is the GET call is looking for the GET_ENTITYSET implementation of whatever value help entity I have added. Since this entity is added programmatically and no CRUD methods generated for this, it is failing to pull data. Framework is working correctly but it is probably not a recommended approach.
